content.text: Adapting a pre-trained language model to a specific task or domain by continuing supervised training on curated demonstration data is one of the most reliable paths from a general-purpose foundation model to a production-ready agent component. The procedure updates model weights on a labeled dataset so that target behaviors—tool-call formats, domain vocabulary, refusal patterns, or response style—become encoded in the network rather than specified in an ever-growing system prompt.
and optimizes on a curated dataset
using the same causal language-modeling loss as pre-training, but applied only to the assistant reply tokens. Prompt tokens are masked so the model learns what to generate, not how to echo the input.
updates every parameter in the model.

—of which Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) is the dominant variant—freezes the original weights and trains tiny low-rank matrices injected beside specific linear layers. Both strategies converge to a task-specialized checkpoint; they differ in compute cost, memory footprint, and peak achievable quality.

General instruction-tuned models are optimized for breadth. They produce grammatically fluent, reasonably safe output across thousands of tasks, but they lack the narrow consistency that production agents require. A customer-support agent that must call a

tool with a precise JSON schema will fail unpredictably if that schema is only described in the system prompt—the model must hold the format reliably across varied user phrasings, edge-case inputs, and multi-turn conversations.

Fine-tuning embeds the target behavior in weights rather than in runtime tokens. This yields several concrete benefits: shorter system prompts (lower inference cost per call), higher format compliance rates, and the ability to distill proprietary multi-step workflows that cannot be expressed in a public prompt without leaking business logic. For agents operating at scale—thousands of calls per minute—even a two-percentage-point improvement in tool-call accuracy can eliminate thousands of retries per day.
— the model reliably produces the exact JSON schema, Markdown structure, or output format your pipeline expects, regardless of prompt variation.
— instructions baked into weights reduce the system prompt by hundreds of tokens, cutting input cost and improving latency.
— internal multi-step procedures can be trained in without appearing in public-facing prompts.
— a fine-tuned 7B model often matches a prompted 70B model on a narrow task, enabling much cheaper production serving.
— legal, medical, or brand-specific language is reliably produced without requiring glossaries in every prompt.
— fine-tune for behavior and format; retrieve for current facts. The two strategies are orthogonal and compose well.
— with fewer than ~100 examples, evaluation loss rises after one or two epochs and generalization degrades.
— aggressive full fine-tuning with a high learning rate can erode general reasoning or safety behaviors established during pre-training and instruction tuning.
— collecting, formatting, and quality-reviewing conversation logs or expert demonstrations is expensive, especially for regulated domains.
— full fine-tuning a 70B model requires hundreds of GB of GPU memory. Even LoRA needs at least one A100 or equivalent; QLoRA reduces this to a single consumer GPU but adds quantization overhead.
— each fine-tuned checkpoint is an artifact that must be stored, versioned, evaluated, and rolled back independently of the base model.
— fine-tuning is poor at injecting large volumes of new factual knowledge. Weights encode behavior, not an easily updatable knowledge base.

The loss is identical to pre-training—cross-entropy over the next-token distribution—but the gradient is zeroed for all prompt positions. Only assistant-turn tokens contribute to the update. Formally, if the concatenated sequence is
is the target response, the supervised fine-tuning loss is:

The model still sees the full context (prompt + prior response tokens) as input, so it learns to attend to the instruction. The mask just ensures the gradient flows only from reply positions, preventing the model from collapsing toward reproducing prompts verbatim.
Every weight matrix in the network is updated via gradient descent. With modern optimizers (AdamW), a low learning rate—typically between

—and one to three epochs suffice for most domain-adaptation tasks. A cosine decay schedule with a short warmup prevents overshooting early in training. Mixed precision (bfloat16 or fp16) keeps memory tractable without sacrificing numerical stability.
LoRA decomposes each weight update into a product of two thin matrices. For a frozen weight

attrs.latex: W = W_0 + \frac{\alpha}{r}\, A B, \quad A \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r},\; B \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times k}

content.text: controls the expressivity of the adapter. Common choices are
targeting the query and value projections of a 7B model, fewer than 0.3% of parameters are trainable. Because
is initialized to zero, the adapter begins as an identity transformation—no random disturbance to the base model at the start of training. The scaling factor
stabilizes the learning rate across different rank choices; setting
makes the effective step independent of

QLoRA combines NF4 quantization of the frozen base weights with 16-bit LoRA adapters. The base occupies roughly 4 bits per parameter, reducing a 7B model to about 4 GB, while the LoRA adapters remain in bfloat16 for gradient precision. Gradient checkpointing stores only selected activations, trading compute for memory. The net result is that a model requiring ~14 GB in fp16 can be fine-tuned on a single 12–16 GB consumer GPU.

The fine-tuning pipeline connects five stages. The pre-trained base model and curated dataset feed into the training stage, which applies either full weight updates or LoRA adapter injection. The resulting fine-tuned model is passed to evaluation; if the model regresses on general tasks or fails task-specific metrics, more data is curated and another training run is launched.
Fine-tuning is the right choice when
The output format is stable and tightly specified (a fixed JSON schema, a particular Markdown structure, or a deterministic tool-call pattern).
Many similar trajectories can be collected: customer support tickets, code review comments, document extraction results—any repeated task with consistent expected outputs.
Latency and cost matter at scale: removing 300 tokens of system-prompt instructions saves real money across millions of API calls.
The required behavior is too complex or too proprietary to describe in a public prompt without leaking confidential business logic.
You want to deploy a smaller model: a fine-tuned 7B can match a prompted 70B on a narrow task, dramatically reducing serving cost.
The task changes rapidly or requirements are not yet stable—data labeling effort is wasted if the target format shifts next month.
Fewer than ~50 examples are available. Prompt engineering and few-shot examples outperform fine-tuning on trivially small datasets.
The goal is knowledge injection—adding an entire product catalog or recent news. RAG handles this better and without staleness.
You are in the prototyping stage. A fine-tuned checkpoint adds a deployment artifact and a re-training loop; validate product-market fit with prompt engineering first.
The implementation breaks into four stages: data preparation, training, evaluation, and deployment. Each stage has common failure modes that are worth guarding against before moving to the next.
— prefer expert-labeled or frontier-model-generated demonstrations over raw logs, which are noisy.
Format with the model's chat template
— every model family uses specific role delimiters and special tokens. Mismatches cause silent format errors.
Set aside a held-out evaluation split
— at least 10–15% of data, stratified by task type. Never tune hyperparameters on this set.
Choose LoRA over full FT unless the task is substantially different from the base instruction distribution
— LoRA is faster, cheaper, and rarely more than 2% behind full FT on narrow tasks.
Monitor train and eval loss per epoch
— stop when eval loss plateaus or starts rising; save checkpoints every epoch.
Evaluate the fine-tuned model against the baseline instruct model
— if the baseline is close, the task may not warrant the overhead.

"""prepare_finetune_data.py

Build a JSONL file of chat examples for supervised fine-tuning.
Each line is one JSON object with a 'messages' list.
"""
import json
from pathlib import Path

SYSTEM = (
    "You are a support agent. When a customer requests a refund, "
    "call the refund_order tool with the order_id and reason."
)

# Raw source data: (user_message, ideal_assistant_response)
raw_pairs = [
    (
        "I'd like to cancel order #4821 because it arrived damaged.",
        '{"tool_call": {"name": "refund_order",'
        ' "arguments": {"order_id": "4821", "reason": "damaged"}}}',
    ),
    (
        "Order 7723 never arrived. Please refund me.",
        '{"tool_call": {"name": "refund_order",'
        ' "arguments": {"order_id": "7723", "reason": "not_delivered"}}}',
    ),
    (
        "Hi, do you have blue hoodies in size M?",
        "I can check stock for you. Could you share the product SKU?",
    ),
    (
        "Wrong item was shipped for order 5512.",
        '{"tool_call": {"name": "refund_order",'
        ' "arguments": {"order_id": "5512", "reason": "wrong_item"}}}',
    ),
]

def make_example(user: str, assistant: str, system: str = SYSTEM) -> dict:
    return {
        "messages": [
            {"role": "system", "content": system},
            {"role": "user", "content": user},
            {"role": "assistant", "content": assistant},
        ]
    }

examples = [make_example(u, a) for u, a in raw_pairs]

# Split 85 / 15 for train / eval
split = int(len(examples) * 0.85)
train, eval_ = examples[:split], examples[split:]

for name, data in [("train", train), ("eval", eval_)]:
    path = Path(f"finetune_{name}.jsonl")
    with path.open("w") as f:
        for ex in data:
            f.write(json.dumps(ex) + "\n")
    print(f"{name}: {len(data)} examples -> {path}")
LoRA fine-tuning with Hugging Face PEFT

"""lora_train.py

Fine-tune a causal LLM with LoRA adapters using Hugging Face Transformers + PEFT.
Requires: pip install transformers peft datasets accelerate bitsandbytes
"""
import torch
from datasets import load_dataset
from peft import LoraConfig, TaskType, get_peft_model
from transformers import (
    AutoModelForCausalLM,
    AutoTokenizer,
    Trainer,
    TrainingArguments,
)

MODEL_NAME = "meta-llama/Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct"  # replace with your base model
MAX_LEN = 512

# 1. Load tokenizer
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(MODEL_NAME)
tokenizer.pad_token = tokenizer.eos_token  # required for batching

# 2. Load base model in bfloat16 to halve memory vs fp32
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    MODEL_NAME,
    torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    device_map="auto",
)

# 3. Inject LoRA adapters — only q_proj and v_proj are trained
lora_cfg = LoraConfig(
    task_type=TaskType.CAUSAL_LM,
    r=8,               # low-rank dimension
    lora_alpha=16,     # effective scale = alpha/r = 2
    target_modules=["q_proj", "v_proj"],
    lora_dropout=0.05,
    bias="none",
)
model = get_peft_model(model, lora_cfg)
model.print_trainable_parameters()  # expect ~0.1-0.3%

# 4. Tokenize: apply chat template and mask prompt tokens with -100
def tokenize(example):
    full = tokenizer.apply_chat_template(
        example["messages"],
        tokenize=True,
        add_generation_prompt=False,
        return_tensors="pt",
    ).squeeze(0)[:MAX_LEN]
    labels = full.clone()
    # Mask everything; unmask from the last assistant token onward
    labels[:] = -100
    # Locate the last occurrence of the assistant role start token
    asst_id = tokenizer.encode("<|assistant|>", add_special_tokens=False)
    for pos in range(len(full) - len(asst_id), -1, -1):
        if full[pos : pos + len(asst_id)].tolist() == asst_id:
            labels[pos + len(asst_id) :] = full[pos + len(asst_id) :]
            break
    return {"input_ids": full, "labels": labels}

train_ds = load_dataset("json", data_files="finetune_train.jsonl", split="train")
train_ds = train_ds.map(tokenize, remove_columns=train_ds.column_names)

# 5. Training — low LR and cosine decay protect general capabilities
args = TrainingArguments(
    output_dir="./lora-checkpoint",
    num_train_epochs=3,
    per_device_train_batch_size=2,
    gradient_accumulation_steps=4,  # effective batch size = 8
    learning_rate=2e-4,
    bf16=True,
    warmup_ratio=0.03,
    lr_scheduler_type="cosine",
    logging_steps=10,
    save_strategy="epoch",
    report_to="none",
)
Trainer(model=model, args=args, train_dataset=train_ds).train()

# 6. Save only adapter weights — base model not duplicated on disk
model.save_pretrained("./lora-adapter")
tokenizer.save_pretrained("./lora-adapter")
print("LoRA adapter saved to ./lora-adapter")

"""evaluate.py

Load a LoRA-fine-tuned model and check it against an eval set.
Prints PASS/FAIL per example and an overall accuracy.
"""
import torch
from peft import PeftModel
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer, pipeline

BASE = "meta-llama/Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct"
ADAPTER = "./lora-adapter"

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(BASE)
base = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(BASE, torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16, device_map="auto")
model = PeftModel.from_pretrained(base, ADAPTER)
model.eval()

gen = pipeline(
    "text-generation",
    model=model,
    tokenizer=tokenizer,
    max_new_tokens=128,
    do_sample=False,  # greedy — deterministic for eval
)

# eval_set: (user message, substring the reply must contain)
eval_set = [
    ("Refund order #9001, product was defective.",  '"refund_order"'),
    ("Order 2244 never arrived.",                   '"not_delivered"'),
    ("Do you ship to Canada?",                      "Canada"),   # should NOT call tool
]

correct = 0
for user_msg, must_contain in eval_set:
    messages = [
        {"role": "system", "content": "You are a support agent."},
        {"role": "user", "content": user_msg},
    ]
    prompt = tokenizer.apply_chat_template(
        messages, tokenize=False, add_generation_prompt=True
    )
    result = gen(prompt)[0]["generated_text"][len(prompt):]
    passed = must_contain.lower() in result.lower()
    print(f"{'PASS' if passed else 'FAIL'}  [{must_contain!r}]")
    print(f"  reply: {result[:100]!r}\n")
    correct += int(passed)

print(f"Accuracy: {correct}/{len(eval_set)}")

A mid-size e-commerce company runs a customer support agent that handles ~80,000 tickets per month. The agent must classify each ticket into one of twelve action categories and emit a structured JSON payload to trigger the relevant backend workflow (refund, reship, escalate, etc.). Their baseline instruct model (GPT-4o-class frontier API) achieved 91% JSON compliance when instructed via a 450-token system prompt. Tool-call errors—invalid fields, wrong order ID format, missing reason codes—caused downstream failures requiring human review.
After collecting 2,400 labeled ticket-resolution examples from senior agents (about two weeks of effort), they fine-tuned a Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct model with LoRA (

, 3 epochs). Results on a 300-example held-out set: JSON compliance rose from 91% (frontier baseline) to 98.7%, with action-category accuracy at 96.2%. System prompt shrunk from 450 to 80 tokens. The 8B fine-tuned model runs on two A10G GPUs at a serving cost approximately 12× lower than the frontier API, with 40 ms median latency versus 1.2 s for the API.
Not masking prompt tokens in the loss
— training on all tokens teaches the model to reproduce the system prompt, wasting gradient capacity and causing repetition at inference.
— Llama 3, Mistral, Qwen, and Phi each use different role tokens. Applying the wrong template produces training data the model cannot parse, ruining format compliance.
Too many epochs on a small dataset
— with 200 examples and 10 epochs, the model memorizes verbatim outputs and fails on paraphrased inputs. Watch eval loss; stop at the minimum.
Learning rate too high for full FT
in full FT cause catastrophic forgetting. LoRA tolerates higher LRs (up to
) because the frozen weights anchor the representation.

— fine-tuning on conversation data should start from an instruct checkpoint, not the raw base model. The base model lacks chat formatting and safety alignment, both of which would need to be re-learned.
No regression testing on general tasks
— a fine-tuned model can gain on the target task while losing safety refusals or multi-step reasoning. Run the base model's eval suite on every checkpoint.
Including PII in training data without review
— models can memorize names, emails, and order IDs from training examples and reproduce them at inference, creating compliance and privacy violations.
Start small: validate the data pipeline and format with 50–100 high-quality examples before labeling thousands. A small, clean dataset outperforms a large noisy one.
Generate synthetic training data from a frontier model at temperature 0 to guarantee consistent format and high quality. Then use the fine-tuned smaller model to serve production traffic.
method rather than manually constructing strings. It handles model-specific delimiters and special token placement automatically.
Save a checkpoint after each epoch and compare eval-set metrics before selecting the production checkpoint. Epoch 2 often beats epoch 3 for small datasets.
Run a canary deployment: shadow-score the fine-tuned model on 5% of live traffic before full rollout, comparing output distributions rather than only test-set accuracy.
Version all training data, hyperparameter choices, and checkpoint names together. When a regression is found, the ability to reproduce a past training run is essential.

The dominant cost axis is GPU memory. Activations during forward and backward passes scale with sequence length and batch size; LoRA's smaller parameter count reduces optimizer-state memory (Adam maintains two moment tensors per trainable parameter). The table below shows approximate peak GPU memory for a 7B-parameter model at batch size 1, gradient accumulation 8, max sequence length 512.

For distributed full fine-tuning, DeepSpeed ZeRO Stage 3 shards optimizer states, gradients, and parameters across GPUs, enabling 70B models to be fine-tuned on 8 × A100-80GB nodes. FSDP (Fully Sharded Data Parallel) in PyTorch provides a similar capability within the native training stack. For LoRA at any scale, gradient checkpointing (recomputing activations rather than storing them) further cuts memory at a 20–30% speed penalty.
The open-source fine-tuning stack has consolidated around Hugging Face Transformers + PEFT for flexibility, with several higher-level frameworks providing opinionated workflows:
— reference implementation of LoRA, QLoRA, prefix tuning, IA³. Integrates directly with Transformers Trainer.
— YAML-driven fine-tuning framework that wires together PEFT, DeepSpeed, and Flash Attention with sensible defaults; popular for quick iteration.
— hand-optimized CUDA kernels that accelerate LoRA training by 2–5× on consumer GPUs; compatible with Hugging Face checkpoints.
— broad model support (LLaMA, Qwen, Mistral, Phi) with built-in dataset formatting, DPO, and reward-model training.
— managed full fine-tuning for GPT-4o-mini and GPT-3.5-Turbo; uploads a JSONL file, trains server-side, returns a custom model identifier. No GPU management required.
— experiment tracking for training loss, eval metrics, and hyperparameter sweeps. Integrates with Trainer via

The decision between prompt engineering, RAG, fine-tuning, and pre-training is one of the most common architectural choices in agent development. Each approach occupies a different position on the effort-versus-flexibility trade-off curve.
Stable format, repeated task, cost at scale
Fine-tuning introduces several risk vectors that are absent when using an off-the-shelf instruct model. They center on the data pipeline, the model artifact, and the deployment environment.

— if training examples are drawn from user-provided text (e.g., customer tickets), an adversary can craft inputs that, when collected and trained on, shift the model's behavior. Audit data sources and apply content filters before training.
— models can extract and repeat strings that appear frequently in training data. Redact or pseudonymize names, email addresses, order IDs, and account numbers before training.

— fine-tuning can reduce the effectiveness of safety refusals established during RLHF. Run the base model's safety benchmark (e.g., HarmBench) on every checkpoint and block any release that regresses below a threshold.
— store checkpoint hashes (SHA-256) alongside model artifacts. Verify on load to detect tampering or corruption during transfer.
— keep the previous production checkpoint accessible for at least 30 days. When a post-deployment regression is found, rollback must be a single deployment command, not another training run.

— LoRA adapters are tiny (a few MB) and do not contain the base model. Ensure your license for the base model permits fine-tuning and serving; many open-weight licenses have commercial-use restrictions.

Think of a pre-trained instruct model as a highly educated generalist who has read widely and can discuss almost anything clearly. Fine-tuning is an apprenticeship: the generalist joins a specific company, follows senior specialists through hundreds of real cases, and gradually internalizes the company's exact processes, terminology, and judgment calls. After the apprenticeship, the specialist handles the company's standard cases faster and more accurately than the generalist ever could from instructions alone—but they are less suited to unrelated tasks the generalist would handle easily.

LoRA refines this analogy: rather than rewriting everything the apprentice knows (full fine-tuning), the company adds a compact reference card—a set of domain-specific heuristics—that the apprentice consults at inference time. The reference card is cheap to produce and swap, but it sits on top of, rather than replacing, the underlying knowledge.
Fine-tuning continues supervised training on curated chat examples; the loss is applied only to assistant-turn tokens, not to the prompt.
LoRA is the practical default: it trains fewer than 1% of parameters, runs on a single A100, and matches full FT quality on most narrow tasks.
QLoRA extends LoRA to consumer GPUs by quantizing frozen base weights to 4 bits while keeping adapters in bfloat16.
A learning rate of 1–5 × 10⁻⁴ for LoRA and 1–5 × 10⁻⁵ for full FT, combined with 1–3 epochs and early stopping, prevents catastrophic forgetting.
Use fine-tuning for stable formats, repeated tasks, and cost at scale. Use RAG for current factual knowledge. Use prompting for prototypes and rare tasks.
Data quality dominates quantity: 200 expert-labeled examples outperform 5,000 noisy logs in most agent fine-tuning scenarios.
Always run safety and general-capability regression tests on every checkpoint; fine-tuning can inadvertently reduce safety alignment.
Redact PII from training data; models memorize and can reproduce identifiers that appear repeatedly across training examples.
